children.name: Manis
children.description: Asian pangolins — four species found across South and Southeast Asia. Pangolins are the only mammals with true scales, made of keratin (the same protein as human fingernails and rhino horns). When threatened, they roll into a tight ball, with the overlapping scales creating near-impenetrable armour — lions and leopards have been recorded failing to unroll pangolins. Pangolins feed exclusively on ants and termites, which they excavate with powerful claws and collect with a tongue longer than their body (which anchors to their pelvis). They have no teeth; their muscular stomach grinds insects with ingested sand and small stones. Asian pangolins are classified as critically endangered — they are the world's most trafficked wild mammals, with an estimated one million taken from the wild in the 2000s for use in traditional medicine and as luxury food.

children.name: Manis crassicaudata
children.description: The Indian pangolin, also called thick-tailed pangolin and scaly anteater, is a pangolin native to the Indian subcontinent. Like other pangolins, it has large, overlapping scales on its body which act as armour. The colour of its scales varies depending on the colour of the earth in its surroundings.

children.name: Smutsia
children.description: African ground pangolins — two species of large, terrestrial pangolins found in sub-Saharan Africa. Temminck's ground pangolin is the most widespread African species, found across the continent south of the Sahara. The giant ground pangolin is the world's largest pangolin, reaching 1.4 m in length. Ground pangolins are primarily terrestrial, walking on their hind legs with their forelegs held away from the body. African pangolins are better studied than Asian species but remain threatened by local hunting and, increasingly, international trafficking to Asia.

children.name: Phataginus
children.description: African tree pangolins — two small, arboreal species found in the rainforests of central and west Africa. Tree pangolins have longer tails relative to body length, with bare scales on the underside of the tail tip that allow gripping branches. The white-bellied pangolin is the most common African species and the most frequently encountered in markets. Tree pangolins are almost entirely nocturnal and arboreal, sleeping in tree hollows by day. Like all pangolins, they are solitary except when mating and when females are caring for young.

children.name: Phataginus tricuspis
children.description: Category:Taxa named by Constantine Samuel Rafinesque.
